name: pyvectorial-au

metadata.name: pyvectorial_au
metadata.summary: Utilities for running, saving, and interfacing with cometary vectorial models, like the one included in sbpy

documentation:
# Pyvectorial
This is a library for interfacing with vectorial models of cometary atmospheres, namely the model included in sbpy, but other versions written in fortran and rust are supported also.
Features include standardized model input and output, database caching of completed models, and parallel running of models.

# Usage

## Coverage Report

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.encoding.encoding_and_hashing.pickle_to_base64
documentation: Serialize an object to a base64 string for storage

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.encoding.encoding_and_hashing.unpickle_from_base64
documentation: Restore object from base64 string encoding

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.encoding.encoding_and_hashing.vmc_to_sha256_digest
documentation: return a string of the sha256 hex digest of the given vmc

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.post_model_processing.fragment_sputter_transform.fragment_sputter_to_cartesian
documentation:
Fragment sputter information is generated by the model in terms of (r, theta, density), so this converts to (x, y, density)
where theta is a spherical polar angle

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.post_model_processing.fragment_sputter_transform.fragment_sputter_to_polar
documentation: Utility function to change the typing of given fragment sputter

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.post_model_processing.fragment_sputter_transform.mirror_fragment_sputter
documentation:
The sputter in (r, theta, density) in spherical coordinate format occupies the positive x-axis only due to the azimuthal symmetry of the problem.
When we convert that to other coordinate systems, we might want to include the negative x-axis by mirroring the fragment sputter around x = 0,
which this function provides.
The return type is the same type as the input

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.haser.haser_fits._haser_column_density
documentation:
Calculates the Haser column density at rho with given parameters to model,
no astropy units attached, all quantities in meters and seconds

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.haser.haser_fits._make_haser_column_density_q
documentation:
Takes HaserParams and returns a column density function with Q as a fitting parameter

Fitting function takes impact parameter in meters and returns column density in 1/m**2

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.tests.test_fortran_version.test_volume_density_grid_first
documentation: def test_num_fragments_theory(vmc_from_fortran):
assert np.isclose(vmc_from_fortran.num_fragments_theory, 0.545e33)
def test_num_fragments_grid(vmc_from_fortran):
assert np.isclose(vmc_from_fortran.num_fragments_grid, 0.533e33)

classes.name: pyvectorial_au.model_output.vectorial_model_calculation.EncodedVMCalculation
documentation:
Uses a pickled VectorialModelResult (using the dill library) because python multiprocessing wants
to pickle return values to send them back to the main calling process.  The coma can't be
pickled by the stock python pickler so we have to encode it ourselves with dill. This allows us
to return this data structure from a job started in parallel, where returning VMCalculation directly
would fail.

classes.name: pyvectorial_au.model_output.vectorial_model_result.VectorialModelResult

class_variables.name: pyvectorial_au.model_output.vectorial_model_result.VectorialModelResult.volume_density
documentation: The fragment sputter that produces the volume density above

class_variables.name: pyvectorial_au.model_output.vectorial_model_result.VectorialModelResult.fragment_sputter
documentation: Some physical quantities of interest

Holds a standardized model result to abstract away the underlying details so that the python,
fortran, and rust models can be run as backends interchangeably

classes.name: pyvectorial_au.model_output.fragment_sputter.FragmentSputterSpherical
documentation:
This holds the direct output of the model's fragment sputter in terms of (r, theta, density)
Theta here is the polar angle in a spherical coordinate system, and the density is azimuthally symmetric
All of the results are then 0 <= theta <= pi, occupying a half-plane
Dataclasses for the two-dimensional fragment sputter information obtained from the model

classes.name: pyvectorial_au.model_output.fragment_sputter.FragmentSputterPolar
documentation:
If we want to expand the fragment density to the whole plane, we use this class to
remove ambiguity of the domain of theta: 0 <= theta <= 2*pi
We can then call mirror_fragment_sputter() to fill in the fragment_density to include
the missing pi <= theta <= 2*pi section of the fragment sputter to have a full-plane
representation

classes.name: pyvectorial_au.model_output.fragment_sputter.FragmentSputterCartesian
documentation:
Representation of the fragment sputter in (x, y, density).  If we take results from
the FragmentSputterSpherical, then only the positive x-axis half of the plane is included.
Like FragmentSputterPolar, we can call mirror_fragment_sputter() to fill in the negative x-axis
to obtain a whole plane view of the fragment density.

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.backends.fortran_version.run_fortran_vectorial_model
documentation: Given a valid FortranModelExtraConfig, runs the vectorial model fortran binary it by sending it the correct keystrokes

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.backends.fortran_version.vmr_from_fortran_output
documentation: Takes the output of the fortran code in fort16_file and returns VectorialModelResult

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.backends.fortran_version.write_fortran_input_file
documentation:
Takes a VectorialModelConfig and produces a fortran input file,
as long as the production is steady

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.backends.python_version.run_python_vectorial_model
documentation:
Takes a VectorialModelConfig and builds the time dependence function
specified, then runs the sbpy vectorial model to return a coma object

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.backends.python_version.vmr_from_sbpy_coma
documentation: Takes a coma object from the sbpy python version and extracts a VectorialModelResult

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.backends.rust_version.run_rust_vectorial_model
documentation: Given path to rust vmodel binary, runs the given model configuration

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.backends.rust_version.vmr_from_rust_output
documentation: Takes the output of the rust model in rust_output_filename and returns VectorialModelResult

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.backends.rust_version.write_rust_input_file
documentation:
Takes a valid vectorial model config and produces a valid input file to give to the rust version of the model as input
Only steady production is currently supported in the rust version

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.db.cache_init.initialize_vectorial_model_cache
documentation:
On startup we call this before any work is done to make sure the db has the proper
tables and columns, and return the full file path for use later

classes.name: pyvectorial_au.model_input.vectorial_model_config.TimeQuantity
documentation: Helper classes for supporting astropy's Quantity values with pydantic validation

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.db.cache_io.get_saved_model_from_cache
documentation:
Given a vectorial model config, look up in the database at vectorial_model_cache_path
whether that config has been run, and return the already-run model if so.

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.db.cache_io.save_model_to_cache
documentation:
Given a completed model, check the given database at vectorial_model_cache_path if the given model
already exists in the database.  If it does not, add it.

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.db.vectorial_model_cache.get_vectorial_model_cache_engine
documentation: Returns an engine to the db based on the given path

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.db.vectorial_model_cache.get_vectorial_model_cache_session
documentation:
Returns a db session based on the given Engine, but if none was given we construct
an engine with the given path.
If an engine is specified, the path is ignored.

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.model_running.vectorial_model_runner.run_vectorial_model_single
documentation:
Given a vectorial model config and database path, look up the result in the database and return
the already-run model.  If it is not in the database, run it and add it to the db.
If no db path is specified, just run the model and return the result.
This was meant to run in its own process through multiprocessing so it must
create its own database engine.

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.model_running.vectorial_model_runner.run_vectorial_models
documentation:
Take a list of model configs and run them in parallel, {parallelism} at a time.

If a cache path is specified, then we only run unique models to avoid trying to
save the same model to the db at the same time

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.model_running.vectorial_model_runner.run_unique_models_only
documentation:
This compares the given vmc_list hashes and runs only the models which are different,
saving the results into the database at vectorial_model_cache_path - no results are returned directly.
This allows us to later "run" the full vmc_list and pull the results out of the database
in the same order.

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.graphing.haser_matplotlib.plot_haser_column_density
documentation: Take HaserParams and plot the column density along the values in the array rs

classes.name: pyvectorial_au.aperture.aperture.UncenteredRectangularAperture

methods.name: pyvectorial_au.aperture.aperture.UncenteredRectangularAperture.shape
documentation: Rectangle dimensions.

Rectangular aperture projected at the distance of the target.

Parameters
----------
shape : `~astropy.units.Quantity`
    A four-element `~astropy.units.Quantity` of angular or
    projected linear size for the width and height of the
    aperture.  The order is not significant.

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.graphing.vm_matplotlib._find_cdens_inflection_points
documentation:
Look for changes in sign of second derivative of the column density,
given a VectorialModelResult and return a list of inflection points

functions.name: pyvectorial_au.graphing.vm_matplotlib.mpl_volume_density_plot
documentation: ax is an axis from a matplotlib figure
